literal: one whose wisdom (*prajñā*) stands firm (*sthita*, past participle of √sthā 'to stand'); a *bahuvrīhi* compound — 'he by whom wisdom has-been-made-firm', i.e. the man of settled, steady wisdom
invariant_definition: *Sthita-prajña* is, as a fixed compound, characteristic of the *Bhagavad-Gītā*, which is its locus classicus: it denotes the person of settled wisdom, one in whom *prajñā* (discerning insight) has become *sthita*, established and no longer shaken by circumstance. It enters the text as the subject of Arjuna's fourfold question (Gītā 2.54: how is the man of steady wisdom described, how does he speak, sit, and move?), and the figure recurs through the *Gītā* under several near-equivalents: *sthita-dhī* ('one of steady intellect', used interchangeably), the *muni* considered under the aspect of inner silence, the *yukta* established in equanimity, and the *brāhmī sthiti*, the brahman-grounded condition (2.72). The description is behavioural — desirelessness, equanimity in pleasure and pain, sense-mastery imaged in the tortoise drawing in its limbs (2.58), and the steadiness of one who, like the ocean filled yet unmoved by entering rivers, takes in all objects without disturbance (2.70). Doctrinally the *sthitaprajña* functions as the *Gītā*'s account of the liberated person who lives and acts in the world, a point at which the ethics of *niṣkāma-karma*, the analysis of sense-mastery, and the soteriology of liberation meet. It is commonly read as the *Gītā*'s counterpart to the Vedāntic *jīvanmukta* (the one liberated while still embodied), and the relation between the two figures is the subject of the school debate over whether the *sthitaprajña* is already liberated or is an advanced practitioner.

school_framing.shared_core: *Sthita-prajña* names **the person whose discerning wisdom has become permanently steady, who lives and acts in the world unmoved by desire, aversion, fear, and grief**. Commentators take the behavioural marks — castoff desire, equanimity in pleasure and pain, tortoise-like sense-withdrawal, and senses governed with the mind fixed on the higher reality — as definitive. They disagree on whether this state is liberation already realized or advanced steadiness on the path, and on what the mind is to be 'fixed upon'.
school_framing.register_axes_note: **Real disagreement** between Advaita, which identifies the *sthitaprajña* with the *jīvanmukta* (liberation already accomplished, the wisdom being identity-knowledge of brahman), and Viśiṣṭādvaita, which reads the figure as a gradation of *jñāna-niṣṭhā* with the mind fixed on the *paramātman* under Bhagavān — steadiness oriented to the Lord, not bare self-isolation. Same ideal-person role, divergent accounts of its content and finality.

per_school.definition: Śaṅkara reads the *sthitaprajña* as the one in whom the discrimination of self and not-self has matured into firm *brahma-jñāna* — effectively the *jīvanmukta*. The casting-off of all mental desires is possible only for one already content in the self by the self, i.e. who knows himself as *brahman*; the behavioural marks describe the lived comportment of such a knower, whose body-mind continues by *prārabdha-karma* while the binding sense of agency and craving has dissolved. The *sthitaprajña* is therefore not someone striving toward liberation but the liberated-in-life person himself, described from the outside.
per_school.register_tag: M+S; jīvanmukta [REAL-DISAGREEMENT: sthitaprajña = jīvanmukta, already free]

per_school.definition: Rāmānuja reads the description as a graded *jñāna-niṣṭhā*: ascending marks of the aspirant fixed in self-knowledge — abandoning desires, unmoved by pleasure and pain, without excessive attachment to fortune or misfortune, and withdrawing the senses tortoise-like. Crucially the mind is not merely isolated in the bare self but disciplined and fixed on the *paramātman* — 'sitting intent on Me' (2.61) — so the steadiness is devotional and Lord-directed. The *sthitaprajña* here is the matured practitioner of disciplined self-knowledge oriented toward Bhagavān, a stage whose consummation is reaching the Lord, not a self-enclosed isolation.
per_school.register_tag: M+S; prapanna [gradation of jñāna-niṣṭhā fixed on paramātman]

per_school.definition: Taken at the level of the text itself, the *sthitaprajña* is the integrating ideal of the *Gītā*'s second chapter and the figure to which its later teaching keeps returning: the person whose wisdom is *pratiṣṭhitā*, who has achieved *prasāda* (serenity) through sense-mastery, and who, like the ocean unmoved by entering rivers, takes in all desires without being disturbed (2.70). The state is named the *brāhmī sthiti*, the brahman-condition, attaining which one is not deluded and reaches *brahma-nirvāṇa* (2.72). It is the bridge between the *Gītā*'s teaching on desireless action and its promise of liberation, and it anticipates the portraits of the *guṇātīta* (14.22–25) and the ideal *bhakta* (12.13–19).

textual_history.summary: *Sthita-prajña* ('one of steady wisdom') is the *Bhagavad-Gītā*'s own coinage: it is not a Vedic or Upaniṣadic term but a compound minted in the *Gītā*'s second chapter, where it is introduced by Arjuna's question and answered across 2.54–72. Its prehistory is not lexical but conceptual — the older idea of *prajñā*, settled insight, and of the *muni* who has mastered the senses. Within the *Gītā* itself the term has a near-synonym, *sthita-dhī* ('of steady intellect'), used interchangeably in the same passage (2.54, 2.56), and it culminates in the *brāhmī sthiti*, the 'Brahman-founded standing' of 2.72. Later Vedānta reads the sthitaprajña as the *jīvanmukta*, the one liberated while living. Honest summary: the word is the *Gītā*'s, and its locus classicus is its only real home; what precedes it is the *concept* of fixed wisdom, not the term.

stages.rendering: What is the description of the man of steady wisdom (sthita-prajña), of one settled in samādhi, Keśava?
stages.note: The term's first appearance — Arjuna's question, which opens the *Gītā*'s portrait of the sage. The same verse already supplies the synonym *sthita-dhī* in Śaṅkara's gloss ('sthita-dhīḥ sthita-prajñaḥ'), the two compounds being treated as one. There is no pre-Gītā attestation of *sthita-prajña*; it is the text's own minting.

stages.sanskrit: prajahāti yadā kāmān sarvān pārtha mano-gatān | ātmany evātmanā tuṣṭaḥ sthita-prajñas tadocyate ||
stages.rendering: When one wholly abandons all the desires that come into the mind, Pārtha, content in the self by the self alone — then is one called of steady wisdom (sthita-prajña).
stages.note: Kṛṣṇa's answer and the term's definition: the sthitaprajña is defined by the abandonment of desire and self-sufficiency in the ātman. The near-synonym recurs at 2.56 ('sthita-dhīr munir ucyate', 'he is called the sage of steady intellect'), binding the term to the *muni*.

stages.sanskrit: eṣā brāhmī sthitiḥ pārtha naināṃ prāpya vimuhyati | sthitvāsyām anta-kāle 'pi brahma-nirvāṇam ṛcchati ||
stages.rendering: This is the Brahman-founded standing, Pārtha; having reached it one is not deluded. Established in it even at the last hour, one attains extinction-into-Brahman.
stages.note: The portrait closes by naming the sthitaprajña's condition the *brāhmī sthiti* — the steady wisdom is a *standing* (sthiti) in Brahman, etymologically echoing the *sthita* of the term. Later Advaita identifies this figure with the *jīvanmukta*, liberated while embodied.

translator_note: 'Person of steady wisdom' or 'one of settled wisdom' is preferable to 'wise man' simpliciter — the load-bearing element is *sthita*, the firmness of the wisdom, not merely its presence. The synonym *sthita-dhī* is identical in force and should not be treated as a separate category. The English should not prejudge the school debate: 'steady wisdom' leaves open whether the figure is the already-liberated *jīvanmukta* (Advaita) or the advanced, Lord-fixed practitioner (Viśiṣṭādvaita), and the term is best understood alongside *jīvanmukta* rather than as a freestanding psychological type.
